name: song-magnetic-compass
description: Chinese navigators adapted the magnetic compass for maritime use during the Song Dynasty, revolutionizing seafaring.

name: song-sternpost-rudder
description: Song Dynasty shipbuilders refined the sternpost rudder, enabling better control of large ocean-going vessels.

name: song-maritime-technology
description: The Song Dynasty built a vast maritime trade fleet that dominated Indian Ocean and Southeast Asian commerce.

name: song-agricultural-treatise
description: Chen Fu's agricultural treatise documented advanced Song Dynasty farming techniques including rice paddy cultivation.

name: bhaskara-mathematics-india
description: Indian mathematician Bhaskara II wrote Siddhanta Shiromani, advancing algebra, calculus concepts, and astronomy.

name: al-idrisi-geography-map
description: Arab geographer al-Idrisi created the Tabula Rogeriana for Sicily's King Roger II, the most accurate world map of its era.

name: song-color-printing
description: Song Dynasty printers developed multi-color printing techniques using separate woodblocks for each color.

name: easter-island-settlement
description: Polynesian voyagers settled Rapa Nui (Easter Island), one of the most remote inhabited islands on Earth.

name: paper-technology-spreads
description: Papermaking technology, originating in China, spread through the Islamic world to reach Europe via Spain and Italy.

name: first-greenhouse
description: Italian monks developed early greenhouse techniques to grow tropical plants in cooler European climates.

name: song-blue-white-porcelain
friendly_name: Song Dynasty Blue and White Porcelain
description: Chinese potters pioneered blue and white porcelain using cobalt pigment, creating one of the most iconic ceramic styles.

name: song-triple-harvest
friendly_name: Song Dynasty Three Harvests per Year
description: Champa rice varieties enabled three annual harvests in southern China, fueling Song Dynasty population growth.

name: potato-cultivation-spread
description: Potato cultivation, first domesticated in the Andes, spread throughout South and Central America as a vital food source.

name: water-mill-expansion
description: Water mills proliferated across medieval Europe, powering grain milling, cloth fulling, and iron forging.

name: windmill-introduction
description: Post-style windmills appeared in northwestern Europe, providing power for grain milling in flat, windy regions.

name: terracing-agriculture-andes
description: Andean civilizations built elaborate agricultural terraces on steep mountain slopes, expanding farmable land.

name: maize-cultivation-expansion
description: Maize cultivation spread from Mesoamerica throughout North and South America, becoming a dietary staple.

name: breast-wheel-design
description: The breastshot water wheel improved on earlier designs by striking the wheel at axle height for greater efficiency.

name: windmill-vertical-axis
description: Post-mills with vertical rotation axes appeared in the Low Countries, becoming iconic features of the Dutch landscape.

name: portolan-chart-development
description: Mediterranean sailors developed portolan charts with compass lines and accurate coastlines, advancing navigation.

name: lance-pike-improvements
description: European armies refined lance and pike designs, making infantry formations more effective against cavalry charges.

name: harrow-development
description: Medieval European farmers developed improved harrow designs for breaking soil clods and preparing seedbeds.

name: potash-production-wood
description: Europeans refined potash extraction from wood ash for use in glassmaking, soap production, and textile bleaching.

name: andean-llama-domestication
description: Andean civilizations perfected llama herding systems for transport, wool, and meat across highland trade networks.

name: compass-adoption-navigation
description: European and Arab sailors adopted the magnetic compass for open-sea navigation across the Mediterranean.

name: astrolabe-navigation
description: The astrolabe became a standard navigation and astronomical tool across the Mediterranean and Islamic world.

name: monsoon-calendar-refinement
friendly_name: Monsoon Calendar Mastery in Islamic Navigation
description: Arab navigators mastered monsoon wind patterns, enabling reliable seasonal trade routes across the Indian Ocean.

name: slash-and-burn-intensive
description: Mesoamerican peoples intensified slash-and-burn farming to support growing populations in tropical forest regions.

name: printing-press-korea
description: Korea produced the first known metal movable type, used to print the Sangjeong Gogeum Yemun anthology.

name: oracle-bones-shang
description: Shang Dynasty diviners inscribed questions on turtle shells and ox bones, creating the earliest Chinese writing.

name: maori-settlement-new-zealand
description: Polynesian voyagers reached Aotearoa (New Zealand), one of the last major landmasses settled by humans.

name: roger-bacon-experimental-method
description: English friar Roger Bacon wrote Opus Majus, advocating empirical observation and experimental methods in science.

name: ancestral-puebloan-drought
description: A severe drought struck the American Southwest, contributing to the abandonment of Ancestral Puebloan cliff dwellings.

name: mechanical-clock-adoption
description: Early mechanical clocks appeared in European churches and monasteries, transforming timekeeping practices.

name: gunpowder-cannons-develop
description: Chinese engineers developed early bronze cannons using gunpowder, the first true firearms in warfare.

name: glass-lens-grinding
description: Italian craftsmen in Venice and Florence refined glass lens grinding, enabling eyeglasses and later optical instruments.

name: scythe-development
description: The long-handled scythe became widely adopted in medieval Europe for efficient grain and hay harvesting.

name: enamel-development-techniques
description: Mediterranean artisans refined vitreous enamel techniques for decorating metalwork, jewelry, and religious objects.

name: blast-furnace-innovation
friendly_name: Blast Furnace Innovation in Medieval Europe
description: European ironworkers developed taller blast furnaces producing cast iron, transforming metallurgy and manufacturing.

name: mali-maritime-expansion
description: Mali Empire ruler Abu Bakr II reportedly launched Atlantic maritime expeditions before Mansa Musa took power.

name: chimu-irrigation-expansion
description: The Chimú civilization built extensive canal irrigation systems along Peru's northern coast, supporting large populations.

name: quarantine-origins
description: Ragusa (Dubrovnik) established a 30-day isolation period for arriving ships, creating the first formal quarantine system.

name: jikji-korea-printing
description: Korean monks printed the Jikji using metal movable type, the oldest surviving book printed with this technology.

name: terrace-farming-andes
description: The Inca expanded terrace farming across the Andes, engineering stepped fields with sophisticated irrigation systems.

name: open-field-system-abandonment
description: England's medieval open field system began declining as landowners consolidated strips into enclosed private farms.

name: manioc-cultivation-caribbean
description: Manioc (cassava) became a primary food staple for Caribbean and South American indigenous populations.

name: kerala-school-mathematics
description: The Kerala school of mathematics in India developed infinite series expansions, anticipating European calculus by centuries.

name: magnifying-glass-single-lens
description: Single convex lenses were refined as magnifying glasses, used by scholars and craftsmen across medieval Europe.

name: star-chart-compilation
description: Islamic astronomers compiled detailed star charts and celestial maps used for both navigation and religious purposes.

name: moldboard-plow-improvements
description: European farmers improved the heavy moldboard plow, enabling more efficient cultivation of dense clay soils.

name: saltpeter-refinement
description: Refinement techniques for saltpeter (potassium nitrate) advanced gunpowder production quality and reliability.

name: soap-making-improvements
description: Mediterranean craftsmen refined soap-making using olive oil and plant ash, producing harder, longer-lasting bars.

name: porcelain-development
description: Chinese potters at Jingdezhen perfected porcelain techniques that made their wares prized trade goods worldwide.

name: ming-treasure-fleets
friendly_name: Zheng He's Treasure Fleet Voyages Begin
description: Admiral Zheng He launched the first of seven massive naval expeditions from China across the Indian Ocean.

name: prince-henry-navigator
description: Prince Henry of Portugal established a navigation center at Sagres, sponsoring expeditions along the African coast.

name: portuguese-caravels
description: Portuguese shipbuilders developed the caravel, a nimble vessel with lateen sails ideal for coastal exploration.

name: astronomical-observatory
description: Timurid ruler Ulugh Beg built a massive observatory in Samarkand, producing the era's most accurate star catalog.

name: ulugh-beg-observatory
description: Ulugh Beg's Samarkand observatory housed a 40-meter sextant and cataloged over 1,000 stars with unprecedented precision.

name: samarkand-observatory
description: The Samarkand observatory, built by Ulugh Beg, became the leading astronomical center of the 15th-century Islamic world.

name: zheng-he-treasure-voyages-end
description: Zheng He's seventh and final voyage concluded, after which China withdrew from large-scale maritime exploration.

name: joseon-sejong-water-clock
description: Korean inventor Jang Yeong-sil built the Jagyeongnu, a self-striking water clock, for King Sejong the Great.

name: caravel-ship-design
description: The caravel's combination of lateen and square sails made it the preferred vessel for Portuguese Age of Discovery voyages.

name: hangul-creation-edict
description: King Sejong the Great of Korea promulgated Hangul, a phonetic alphabet designed to improve literacy among commoners.

name: joseon-scientific-innovation
description: The Joseon Dynasty under King Sejong produced rain gauges, sundials, and celestial globes advancing Korean science.

name: eyeglass-improvements
description: Venetian glassmakers improved eyeglass lenses for both near- and farsightedness, expanding their practical use.

name: armillary-sphere-korean
description: Korean astronomers built refined armillary spheres to model celestial movements during King Sejong's reign.

name: printing-press-diffusion
description: Gutenberg's printing press technology spread rapidly across Europe, with print shops in most major cities by 1480.

name: inca-terrace-agriculture
description: The Inca Empire's terrace agriculture reached its peak, supporting millions across the Andes mountain range.

name: dias-cape-rounding
friendly_name: Dias Rounds Cape of Good Hope
description: Portuguese explorer Bartolomeu Dias became the first European to sail around the southern tip of Africa.

name: end-medieval-period-1500
description: By 1500, over 20 million volumes had been printed in Europe, fundamentally transforming knowledge dissemination.

name: polynesian-settlement-expansion
description: Polynesian navigators continued settling remote Pacific islands using star navigation and ocean current knowledge.

name: melanesian-settlement
description: Melanesian peoples maintained complex trade networks and settlement patterns across islands of the western Pacific.

name: anatomical-drawing
description: Leonardo da Vinci began detailed anatomical drawings from human dissections, advancing understanding of the body.

name: sugar-plantation-establishment
description: Spanish colonists established sugar plantations in the Caribbean, beginning a system reliant on enslaved labor.

name: coffee-cultivation-expansion
friendly_name: Coffee Cultivation Expands in Ottoman Empire
description: Coffee cultivation expanded from Ethiopia and Yemen throughout the Ottoman Empire, with coffeehouses spreading rapidly.

name: enclosure-movement-begins
description: English landowners began enclosing common fields for sheep grazing, displacing peasant farmers from the land.

name: nautical-table-creation
description: Portuguese navigators compiled nautical tables and ephemerides for calculating latitude at sea using celestial observations.

name: arquebus-musket-development
description: Spanish armorers refined the arquebus into a reliable infantry weapon that transformed European battlefield tactics.

name: rifling-development
description: German gunsmiths developed spiral grooves inside gun barrels to spin projectiles, improving accuracy.

name: scratch-plow-design
description: Early farmers in the Fertile Crescent developed the scratch plow (ard), one of the first tools to break soil for planting.

name: varnish-composition-formulas
description: European craftsmen developed refined varnish formulas using resins and oils for protecting wood and paintings.

name: enclosure-movement
description: The enclosure movement consolidated open fields into private holdings, boosting productivity but displacing rural poor.

name: printing-impact-literacy
description: The spread of printed books dramatically increased literacy rates across Western Europe within two generations.

name: literacy-expansion-post-printing
description: Affordable printed materials enabled wider access to knowledge, fueling the Renaissance and Reformation movements.

name: first-autopsy
description: Leonardo da Vinci performed detailed human dissections and autopsies, recording findings in meticulous anatomical drawings.

name: copernican-astronomy
description: Copernicus published De Revolutionibus, proposing a heliocentric model that placed the Sun at the center of the cosmos.

name: copernican-revolution
description: Nicolaus Copernicus published his heliocentric theory, fundamentally challenging the geocentric worldview.

name: chocolate-europe
description: Kekchi Maya nobles presented chocolate as a drink to Spanish Prince Philip, introducing cacao to European courts.

name: botanical-garden
description: The Orto Botanico di Padova was founded as the world's first university botanical garden for studying medicinal plants.

name: scientific-revolution-dawn
description: The publication of works by Copernicus and Vesalius in 1543 is often cited as the start of the Scientific Revolution.

name: maize-introduction-africa-asia
friendly_name: Maize Introduced to Africa and Asia
description: Portuguese traders introduced American maize to Africa and Asia, where it became a major food crop within decades.

name: printing-press-standardization
description: Printing technology standardized across Europe with uniform typefaces, page formats, and publishing conventions.

name: printing-technological-lock
description: The printing press became the dominant information technology in Europe, replacing manuscript copying entirely.

name: potato-introduction-europe
description: Spanish explorers brought potatoes from South America to Europe, where they gradually became a staple crop.

name: tycho-brahe-astronomical-observations
description: Danish astronomer Tycho Brahe built Uraniborg observatory and made the most precise pre-telescope star measurements.

name: compound-microscope
description: Dutch spectacle makers Hans and Zacharias Janssen built the first compound microscope using multiple lenses.

name: first-thermometer
description: Galileo Galilei invented an early thermoscope that measured temperature changes using air expansion in water.

name: new-world-crops-adoption
friendly_name: Adoption of New World Crops in Old World
description: Crops from the Americas including tomatoes, peppers, and squash became integrated into Eurasian and African diets.

name: coffee-introduction
description: Venetian merchants brought coffee to Europe from Ottoman markets, sparking the continent's coffeehouse culture.

name: scientific-instruments-development
description: European instrument makers developed precision tools including telescopes, microscopes, and barometers for scientific inquiry.

name: cotton-production-india
description: India's cotton textile industry dominated global trade, producing finely woven fabrics exported across Asia and Europe.

name: compound-lens-system
description: Dutch opticians developed compound lens systems combining multiple lenses, enabling microscopes and telescopes.

name: cartridge-paper-development
description: Paper cartridges combining powder and ball were developed, speeding up musket reloading on the battlefield.

name: sulfuric-acid-production
description: German alchemists developed early methods for producing sulfuric acid, a key industrial chemical for centuries to come.

name: glass-composition-improvements
description: Venetian glassmakers achieved exceptional clarity and purity in cristallo glass, prized throughout Europe.

name: printing-technology-spread
description: Printing technology spread beyond Europe to the Ottoman Empire, India, and the Americas via missionary and trade networks.

name: sugar-monoculture-caribbean
description: Sugar monoculture dominated Caribbean islands, driving the transatlantic slave trade and reshaping colonial economies.

name: tokugawa-rice-standardization
description: The Tokugawa shogunate standardized rice measurement as the basis of Japan's feudal tax and economic system.

name: hudson-explorations
description: English explorer Henry Hudson made four voyages seeking a northern passage, exploring the Arctic and northeastern America.

name: virginia-colony-established
description: English settlers founded Jamestown, Virginia, the first permanent English colony in the Americas.

name: telescope-invention
description: Dutch spectacle maker Hans Lippershey applied for the first telescope patent, sparking a revolution in astronomy.

name: kepler-planetary-laws
description: Johannes Kepler published his first two laws of planetary motion, showing planets orbit in ellipses, not circles.

name: keplers-laws
description: Kepler's Astronomia Nova introduced elliptical orbits and the equal-area law, transforming celestial mechanics.

name: first-telescope
description: Galileo built an improved telescope and turned it skyward, discovering Jupiter's moons, lunar craters, and sunspots.

name: kepler-laws-motion
friendly_name: Johannes Kepler's Laws of Planetary Motion
description: Kepler derived three fundamental laws describing planetary motion from Tycho Brahe's observational data.

name: tea-introduction
description: Dutch traders brought Chinese tea to Europe, beginning a trade that would reshape global commerce and culture.

name: galileo-telescopic-observations
description: Galileo published Sidereus Nuncius describing telescopic discoveries including Jupiter's moons and lunar mountains.

name: galileo-telescope-discoveries
description: Galileo's telescopic observations provided key evidence for the Copernican heliocentric model of the solar system.
